definition: The opacity of private credit markets systematically biases observable risk indicators toward false optimism. The direction of this bias is certain but the magnitude is uncertain, creating underappreciated systemic risk.
evidence: No historical analogue at this scale
Base rate framework from 5 analogues (S&Ls, SIVs, junk bonds, Japanese jusen, AT1) shows 60-80% disorderly repricing rate with 4-5 year median lag
Three spillover channels: BDC/interval fund redemptions (~$300B), insurance RBC (~$700B), information contagion
Mark-to-recovery gap could be 5% or 25% — too wide to be actionable
Mark-to-model NAVs suppress measured volatility by 2-3x
PIK toggle usage rising from ~2% to 5-8%
Credit impulse R² declined from 0.38 to 0.12, partially attributable to $200B→$1.7T private credit growth
Six agents converge on direction of bias
15-25% portfolio volatility underestimation figure is analytically derived but not empirically validated
Asset class has never been tested through a full cycle at this scale
open_questions: Does private credit opacity create systemic risk or is it contained?
What is the actual mark-to-recovery gap?
Is there a true historical analogue at $1.7T scale?
What is the actual default rate vs reported in private credit?
What is the actual magnitude of volatility suppression vs the analytically derived 15-25%?
What is the loss absorption capacity of insurance companies and pension funds for private credit losses?
At what scale do BDC/interval fund redemptions ($300B), insurance RBC impairment ($700B), or information contagion create systemic spillover?
